mnemonics.title: Array Index Rule
mnemonics.content: FIRST ELEMENT IS AT ZERO: Remember—arrays in C start counting from 0, not 1. The first student in a class is at seat 0, not seat 1!

mnemonics.title: Bounds Checking
mnemonics.content: NO BOUNDS CHECKING IN C: C trusts you completely. If you walk off the array edge, C won't stop you—you might fall into garbage memory or crash. Be careful!

mnemonics.title: Array Size Calculation
mnemonics.content: COUNT ELEMENTS: sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) gives the number of elements. Think: TOTAL PIZZA SLICES ÷ SLICE SIZE = NUMBER OF SLICES.

mnemonics.title: 2D Array Memory
mnemonics.content: ROW-MAJOR ORDER: Rows sit side by side like pages in a book. Row 0 complete, then Row 1, then Row 2—all in one continuous block.

acronyms.expansion: Arranged Collection of Related Items Yearly Stored
acronyms.usage: Helps remember that arrays store multiple related items of the same type
Array whose size is determined at runtime (C99 feature)
Not for arrays directly, but helps understand stack vs heap memory

visualTips.visualization: Think of an array as a hotel floor. Room numbers start at 0. Room 0 is first, Room 1 is second. Each room (element) has same size, all connected in a row. You can jump directly to any room by knowing its number.
Draw boxes in a single row: [10][20][30][40][50]. Each box represents one element at consecutive addresses. The array name points to the first box's address.
A 2D array is like a seating chart. Students[row][column]. First index is row (vertical), second is column (horizontal). Row 0 is first row, Column 0 is first column.
Draw arr as an arrow pointing to first element box. Adding 1 to pointer (arr+1) moves to next box. *arr retrieves what's inside the first box.

analogies.analogy: Declaring int arr[5] is like buying 5 lockers numbered 0-4. You know you have exactly 5 spots ready to store integer values.
Array elements are like houses on a single street—each address is next to the other. This neighborhood arrangement lets you find any house quickly by street number.
Array size is like the number of seats on a bus—fixed when the bus is built. You cannot add more seats while driving; you need a bigger bus (new array).
Think of array indices like elevator floor buttons in some countries—ground floor is 0, first floor is 1. The first element lives on floor 0.
